title: Singles Week

jokes.text: I just overheard three women with smokers' coughs talking about 'Sex in the City'.
jokes.setup: Moira announces the singles have arrived for Singles Week.
jokes.punchline: Her description of them — smokers' coughs and mispronouncing 'Sex and the City' — is simultaneously dismissive and affectionate, perfectly capturing Moira's class-conscious worldview.
jokes.explanation: The 'Sex in the City' mispronunciation is a layered joke: it signals the kind of people attending, does so through Moira's filter of barely-concealed snobbery, and uses an instantly recognizable cultural shorthand. High character integration because only Moira would notice and report this detail with this framing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The singles have arrived. I can tell by the distinctive timbre of their smokers' coughs and their passionate debate about which one of them is the Carrie.

jokes.text: Darling, Mommy's delivering the keynote address. There's going to be press and photographers there, I think it would be a tad déclassé for the chair of the event to be wearing a Volunteers t-shirt.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks why Moira isn't wearing the event volunteer t-shirt like everyone else.
jokes.punchline: Moira explains with theatrical grandeur why wearing a volunteer t-shirt would be beneath her, while calling herself 'chair' despite being co-chair.
jokes.explanation: Moira calling a small-town Singles Week keynote a matter of press and photographers is the perfect expression of her delusion. 'Déclassé' used about a volunteer t-shirt is spot-on Moira vocabulary. High character integration; this joke only works coming from her.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not wearing that t-shirt. Do you know what kind of message that sends? That I'm a volunteer. I didn't come here to volunteer, I came here to be seen volunteering.

jokes.text: Mkay, I'm co-chair, and I'm wearing my t-shirt.
jokes.setup: Moira has just made an elaborate case for why she can't wear the volunteer t-shirt as 'chair.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis points out she's also co-chair and is wearing hers — deflating Moira's pretension with simple logic.
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical deflation of Moira's grandiosity. The comedic value is in the simplicity of Alexis catching the flaw in Moira's logic without drama. Works well as a comic beat but not especially original.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, darling, I understand you're upset, but let's not throw the baby out with the bathwater. We need to stay in our lane here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, that makes no sense. You're the one who—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to stop you right there. You see, in my day, we understood the concept of teamwork. We didn't just go rogue and fumble the ball like some sort of... pedestrian amateur.

jokes.text: Love is definitely in the air! Motel at max capace all week long.
jokes.setup: John (Johnny) and Alexis are having a mild argument about the event.
jokes.punchline: Moira cheerfully pivots to celebrating the motel being at capacity — connecting 'love in the air' to the financial upside for the business, revealing her priorities.
jokes.explanation: Moira's use of 'capace' (likely 'capacità' mangled into a Moira-ism) and her breezy pivot to the motel business while pretending to be romantic is a nice character beat. Mild but consistent with her voice.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, after the success of our last co-chaired event, I thought we'd tackle something truly meaningful: a formal ball celebrating the ravages of adolescent skin conditions.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, no.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The Rosemont Luxury Hotel has graciously offered their grand ballroom. We'll have ice sculptures shaped like blackheads, and the silent auction will feature skincare products that haven't worked on anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, why would anyone want to go to that?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because, darling, it's ironic. And nothing says 'black tie optional' like commemorating the worst years of everyone's lives in a five-star setting.

jokes.text: I appreciate everything you do, Alexis, but I think it's best I carry the baton over the goal line.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to diplomatically justify cutting Alexis out of the event.
jokes.punchline: She mixes metaphors ('baton' from a relay race, 'goal line' from football) while pretending to be gracious — the malapropism of idioms reveals her insincerity.
jokes.explanation: The mixed-sports-metaphor is a reliable Moira character beat — she speaks with false authority while getting things subtly wrong. Functional comedy, well-integrated into character, but not a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira Rose
dialogue.line: They're like raspy-voiced Miranda Hobbes types.

jokes.text: Do you remember the last time you and I chaired an event? The Teenage Rosacea Ball at the St. Regis? / That was 15 years ago!
jokes.setup: Moira is justifying her takeover of the event by referencing their shared history of collaborating.
jokes.punchline: The event she cites — 'The Teenage Rosacea Ball at the St. Regis' — is such a specific, bizarre detail from the Roses' former wealthy life that it's inherently funny, and Alexis's defensive response acknowledges how long ago it was.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Teenage Rosacea Ball at the St. Regis' is peak Schitt's Creek world-building comedy — it's absurd enough to be funny on its own (a ball for teens with a skin condition, at a luxury hotel) and simultaneously reveals the Roses' ridiculous former life. High originality and character integration. Quotable and rewards repeat viewings for how casually it's dropped.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Hold on, hold on. Yeah, I'm in a meeting right now. Yeah, with Johnny Rose. No, no, he's getting really mad. His face is turning red. Now he's standing up. Oh, he's pointing at me. Yeah, I can see that, he's pointing right at me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: He just yelled my name. Yeah, just my name. Very forceful. Oh, now he's doing that thing where he closes his eyes and pinches the bridge of his nose.

jokes.text: I'm about to swing by Jocelyn's and get the locks and keys for this morning's event. / You still haven't picked up the locks and keys?
jokes.setup: Moira has been defending her competence and leadership, positioning herself as essential to the event.
jokes.punchline: She immediately reveals she hasn't done the most basic logistical task yet — getting the supplies.
jokes.explanation: A clean irony beat — Moira's competence claims are immediately undercut by her incompetence. Well-structured deflation, though the joke type (self-important character reveals failure) is familiar sitcom territory.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, no, he gets very angry. I've seen it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: One time he yelled at me for ten minutes straight because I parked in his spot.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm standing right here, Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know, Johnny. That's why I'm being so honest.

jokes.text: I just walked past three very raspy-voiced Miranda's outside, so I think the singles are here.
jokes.setup: The motel team is waiting for singles to arrive.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan description of the attendees as 'raspy-voiced Mirandas' (a Sex and the City character) callbacks Moira's earlier 'Sex in the City' joke while adding Stevie's signature dry wit.
jokes.explanation: This is a callback that improves on the original — Stevie gets the show's name right ('Miranda' instead of the whole title) and delivers it with perfect deadpan. The contrast between Moira's theatrical announcement and Stevie's flat report makes both funnier. Strong character integration for Stevie.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What else do you need?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I need to finalize a few things with the real estate agent, I need to call the accountant...

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And maybe a brow trim?

jokes.text: Okay, with the 50% premium charge for Singles Week, I think we might be able to afford some of the bigger improvements we've been talking about.
jokes.setup: Johnny is looking at the financial upside of Singles Week.
jokes.punchline: The fact that a 50% premium charge — essentially price-gouging lonely people — is being described in the most wholesome, optimistic terms possible is the understated joke.
jokes.explanation: A mild ironic beat — charging singles a premium to find love has an inherent dark comedy to it that the show doesn't fully exploit here. Johnny plays it straight. Functional scene-setting rather than a strong joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This box is just... it's an awkward shape.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, your struggle with it has been absolutely effortless to watch.

jokes.text: Roland's phone. / Roland, hang up. / Yeah, uh, Johnny's tellin' me I gotta hang up. Yes, they're both here, staring at me. Johnny has that upset look on his face.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie arrive for an important meeting and Roland answers his phone.
jokes.punchline: Roland narrates Johnny's reaction TO the phone call for Bob while Johnny stands right there, making the transgression worse in real time.
jokes.explanation: Roland's obliviousness is perfectly captured — he isn't sneaking the call, he's describing the situation TO the caller while the situation unfolds. The escalating rudeness (staying on the phone while describing Johnny's reaction) is a precise character beat that makes the joke land cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not struggling. I'm just... repositioning.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And thank you, I have been going to the gym.

jokes.text: Okay, I'm gonna put this on silent for the rest of the meeting, okay? Do you know how to do that, Stevie?
jokes.setup: Roland has finally been pressured to get off his phone during the meeting.
jokes.punchline: He asks STEVIE — the young, tech-savvy motel manager — if she knows how to put a phone on silent, implying he doesn't know but also condescendingly directing the question to her.
jokes.explanation: Roland asking Stevie to silence his own phone is a good character beat — it reveals his technological incompetence while also setting up the later plot point about the missed calls. Efficient double duty for the joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't deliver the box because emotional vulnerability is cringe-y, and frankly, I'm not going to subject myself to that kind of public humiliation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Besides, I already told Patrick everything about my feelings anyway, so there's nothing left to spill.

jokes.text: How 'bout a car wash? Or maybe a brow trim for Johnny?
jokes.setup: Johnny has given Roland a task list of errands to run, ending with 'anything else you want, just let me know.'
jokes.punchline: Roland takes the offer literally and suggests personal grooming tasks for Johnny himself.
jokes.explanation: Roland subverts Johnny's polite offer by treating it as an invitation to make personal observations. The 'brow trim' specificity is funny — it's a particular enough insult to suggest Roland has thought about it before. Good economy and character work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, you have to deliver this box. It's very important.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, why can't you deliver it?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because I'm... I'm busy. And you're going in that direction anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm really not.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you will be, once you agree to deliver this box. Which means you're already going in that direction in your mind, which is the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not how directions work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It is though, because if you think about going somewhere, you've already mentally gone there, so your body might as well follow, and at that point, you're obligated.

jokes.text: David struggles to carry a large, awkwardly shaped box out of the store, grunting with effort.
jokes.setup: David needs to carry a box of dog sweaters to Ted's clinic.
jokes.punchline: David, famously averse to physical effort, is audibly struggling with what he'll later describe as a 'very awkwardly shaped box.'
jokes.explanation: Visual/physical comedy that works because of David's character — his physical exertion is always comedic given his disdain for effort. The setup is physical, the payoff is in his self-conscious acknowledgment. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You just told me you love me, and now you're telling me this is going to stress me out?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Those two things are in direct conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: You been goin' to the gym, 'cause that looked effortless?
jokes.setup: David has just visibly struggled to carry a box through the door, grunting and struggling.
jokes.punchline: Patrick deadpans that it 'looked effortless,' the opposite of what just happened, delivered as a sincere compliment.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's deadpan sarcasm delivered as sincere observation is a hallmark of their dynamic. The economy here is nearly perfect — seven words that are the exact opposite of reality, delivered straight. It's the kind of line that rewards the pairing: Patrick teasing David's vanity while David's ego still accepts the framing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've only said 'I love you' to my parents twice, and once to Mariah Carey.

jokes.text: It's just a very awkwardly shaped box, but yes I have, and thank you for noticing.
jokes.setup: Patrick has sarcastically complimented David's gym progress after the box struggle.
jokes.punchline: David first defends the box's shape, but then accepts the gym compliment earnestly — his vanity overrides his awareness that he's being teased.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautiful character comedy beat. David first defends himself (the box is awkward, not him) but then pivots to accepting a compliment that was clearly sarcastic. The self-awareness/lack of self-awareness tension is essential David. 'Thank you for noticing' particularly earns a high character integration score — only David Rose would respond to obvious teasing with genuine gratitude for the embedded flattery.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I... I love you too. And you know how I feel about live music, and crowds, and outdoor venues, and most forms of human expression, but I would go to a Mariah Carey concert with you.

jokes.text: I was just wondering what your day was looking like? / I'm not dropping the box off for you.
jokes.setup: David awkwardly mentions what his day looks like, clearly building to asking Patrick a favor.
jokes.punchline: Patrick cuts him off before he can even finish the sentence, demonstrating he knows David so well he can read the manipulation coming.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the precision of Patrick's preemptive refusal — he knows David's manipulative build-up so well he doesn't let him complete it. This is excellent character integration and structure: the setup barely begins before the punchline lands, which makes it funnier than if David had gone further. The intimacy of their relationship is the comedic engine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can manage this myself. It's just a box.

jokes.text: The whole situation with Alexis and Ted right now is very awkward and cringe-y.
jokes.setup: David is explaining why he doesn't want to deliver the box to Ted himself.
jokes.punchline: David using 'cringe-y' as a clinical term for the emotional situation — reducing his sister's heartbreak to a social inconvenience — is entirely on-brand.
jokes.explanation: David's reduction of his sister's emotional vulnerability to 'awkward and cringe-y' is a consistent character note. Functional, reveals character, but not a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, that was a thing.

jokes.text: Alexis told him she loved him. [beat] Wow. Well, good for her, it's... it's not easy puttin' yourself out there like that. — Okay, sure.
jokes.setup: David reveals that Alexis told Ted she loved him, which is why things are awkward
jokes.punchline: Patrick's genuine warmth ('good for her') is undercut by David's flat 'Okay, sure' — David can't engage with sincerity right now, and his deflection is the joke
jokes.explanation: The 'Okay, sure' is doing a lot: David's discomfort with sincere emotion, his anxiety about the Ted situation, and a hint that the conversation is moving toward territory he's about to be very vulnerable in himself. Rewatch value because of what follows.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can't get the keys because I'm wearing press-on nails and I just had them applied at a length that makes it impossible for me to perform basic motor functions. I can't open doors, I can't operate a telephone, and I certainly can't be expected to retrieve keys from a keyring like some sort of common laborer.

jokes.text: The whole situation with Alexis and Ted right now is very awkward and cringe-y.
jokes.setup: David is explaining why he can't face Ted directly
jokes.punchline: David using 'cringe-y' as a descriptor — slightly self-aware, and the understatement of 'awkward and cringe-y' for a deeply emotionally complex situation
jokes.explanation: Serviceable character moment — David reducing emotional complexity to 'cringe-y' is on-brand. Not a standout joke but a reliable character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You're doing beautifully, Jocelyn. Just breathe through the contractions. In and out, in and out.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I can't do this, Moira!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, you can. You're a strong woman. Although I must say, this little frippit is certainly making quite the entrance. Rather inconsiderate timing, if you ask me. We have the gala tomorrow evening.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And frankly, I'm not entirely convinced this baby is media-ready. Has it even considered its close-up?

jokes.text: What you're doing is very brave. Very generous, okay? And I don't wanna add more stress to your day, but I love you.
jokes.setup: Patrick has been building David up, saying his errand-running is brave and generous. It sounds like a pep talk.
jokes.punchline: The pep talk was a setup for Patrick to drop 'I love you' for the first time — using the compliments as a buffer for the most significant romantic declaration in the series to this point.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke/moment of the episode and among the best romantic comedy beats in the series. The structure is exquisite: Patrick weaponizes the format of a supportive pep talk to deliver the most emotionally loaded line possible, then immediately acknowledges it will make David's day MORE stressful. It's simultaneously funny and deeply romantic — rare combination. The joke requires the full weight of their relationship to land, making it exceptionally earned. The best moment in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God! We need to get to the hospital!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Moira, she's literally driving us there right now.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm very nervous! What if something goes wrong? What if I say the wrong thing?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're not the one having the baby.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm aware, but my nerves are very delicate!

jokes.text: So you just said that to me for the first time, knowing that it would make my day more stressful. That's correct.
jokes.setup: Patrick has just said 'I love you' for the first time, prefacing it by acknowledging it would add stress.
jokes.punchline: David's processing — framing the declaration as a timing problem — and Patrick's unapologetic confirmation is both funny and sweet.
jokes.explanation: David's instinct to process a love confession as a scheduling/logistics problem is perfectly in character. Patrick's flat 'That's correct' is even better — he planned this, he knew what he was doing, and he's not sorry. The exchange shows both characters perfectly: David can't just feel a feeling without intellectualizing it, and Patrick finds this endearing enough to work with. Exceptional character integration for both.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, whose phone is this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know, it won't stop ringing.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, answer it!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Hello? Hello, yes, she's— no, no, this isn't— I'm not— I'm not Tom.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What? What are they saying?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I think... I think his wife is in labor.

jokes.text: Because you know that I've never said that to anyone else, aside from my parents twice, and one time at a Mariah Carey concert, I know. Yeah.
jokes.setup: David is processing that Patrick said 'I love you' knowing David had never said it before.
jokes.punchline: David's qualifications of how many times he's said 'I love you' — twice to parents, once at a Mariah Carey concert — reveals both his emotional walls and his specific brand of cultural devotion. Patrick already knowing all this adds another layer.
jokes.explanation: The Mariah Carey concert qualification is a perfect piece of character comedy — absurd and completely specific to David. It's funny because it's exactly the kind of thing that would be true about him (Mariah as a quasi-religious experience), because it's a bizarre exception to the rule, and because Patrick already knows it and finishes the thought. The line does enormous character work in very few words. High quotability because 'aside from my parents twice, and one time at a Mariah Carey concert' is a complete comedy universe in one clause.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: The progression from words to baby emojis to explosion emoji

jokes.text: You're my Mariah Carey.
jokes.setup: Patrick has said he doesn't expect David to say 'I love you' back right now, and that it felt right to him in the moment.
jokes.punchline: David, unable to say 'I love you,' offers the highest compliment in his personal vocabulary: comparing Patrick to Mariah Carey.
jokes.explanation: This is the best line in the episode and one of the great romantic one-liners in Schitt's Creek. It works on every level simultaneously: it's funny because Mariah Carey is an absurd frame of reference for romantic devotion; it's emotionally resonant because David IS saying 'I love you' in the only language he knows how; it's perfect character comedy because ONLY David Rose would express love this way; and it's the perfect callback to the Mariah concert detail just established. Economy is flawless — four words that contain an entire romantic declaration. This approaches the caliber of the Snip Snap anchor, though it's sweeter and less chaotic.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, if you hadn't turned off the phone, we wouldn't be in this situation.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Me? You're the one who can't figure out how to use the TV remote. You think the internet lives in the router.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's completely different.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Is it though? Because last week you asked me if you could download more RAM.

jokes.text: Okay. That compliment could bring me to tears, but I'm not gonna let it.
jokes.setup: David has just said 'You're my Mariah Carey.'
jokes.punchline: Patrick acknowledges it's genuinely moving — but maintains composure — which validates the absurdity of the Mariah compliment while revealing his genuine emotion.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's response is the perfect button to the Mariah moment. By saying it could bring him to tears, he signals that he understood what David meant — that it was a real declaration of love — while keeping his composure in a way that is classically Patrick. The contrast between the absurdity of the reference and Patrick's sincere emotional response is perfectly calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You know, I have to say, I'm a little miffed. These dogs are getting cashmere sweaters and I don't even get a Christmas gift.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Ted, you work at the same office as me. I see you every day.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I guess you could say this is really getting to my... pet peeves.

jokes.text: Um, would you mind just not looking at me while I take this box outta the room, 'cause I don't want you regretting any of the nice things that you just said to me?
jokes.setup: The emotional moment has concluded and David needs to leave with the box.
jokes.punchline: David's anxiety — that Patrick will watch him struggle with the box and reconsider his romantic declarations — is vulnerable and funny simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: David's fear that watching him carry the box badly will undo Patrick's love confession is absurd and deeply in character — he's so convinced his vulnerability will be punished that he needs literal blindness from Patrick. The line is both funny and quietly revealing. 'Don't want you regretting any of the nice things you just said to me' is particularly quotable and shows David still half-expecting rejection even in the warmth of the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Your presence has thrown me off my game.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think your game is very... tight.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Very snug.

jokes.text: That went well.
jokes.setup: David has just exited awkwardly, asking Patrick not to look at him.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's dry two-word summary to himself — after an emotional first 'I love you' followed by an anxious exit — is the perfect button.
jokes.explanation: Two words that contain the entirety of Patrick's relationship with David — affectionate, amused, and slightly bemused. The deadpan understatement after an emotionally loaded scene is classic Patrick and classic Schitt's Creek tonal management. Economy score of 10: two words, maximum impact.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, if it makes you feel any better, that's not normal for her.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I once saw her cry at a store when they didn't have her shoe size, and she just... moved on with her life.

jokes.text: [Patrick watches David struggle out the door] That went well.
jokes.setup: Patrick has just watched the most emotionally loaded exit in the show's history
jokes.punchline: 'That went well' — Patrick's signature understatement, clearly to himself, as a button on the entire Patrick/David love scene
jokes.explanation: Three words doing enormous work. 'That went well' — after David asked him not to look, then clearly looked, while David struggled with the box after a declaration of love — is the perfect Patrick deadpan. It's also genuinely ambiguous: Patrick IS satisfied with how it went, emotionally. The understatement is both funny and touching. Economy score of 10 is justified.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I think that's everything. I should probably get going.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, actually, before you go — I'm engaged.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: Jocelyn is in labor! / Oh my God! / I know it's no one's fault.
jokes.setup: Moira calls Alexis to explain why she can't get the keys and locks.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately pre-absolves everyone of blame ('I know it's no one's fault') for Jocelyn going into labor — as if fault is a reasonable framework for a natural birth.
jokes.explanation: Moira framing a childbirth as a fault-assignable event is a good character beat. Her reflexive PR instinct — get ahead of blame — applied to labor is absurd and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that you broke up with Heather? And now you need to have a feelings conversation about it?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm going to need a moment to process the fact that I'm now emotionally invested in your relationship status.

jokes.text: Oh! Don't start without me you little frippit. You don't have the media training.
jokes.setup: Moira is telling Alexis to wait for her before starting the Singles Week event.
jokes.punchline: 'Frippit' is a Moira-invented insult (or archaic term of endearment used as an insult), and invoking 'media training' for a small-town Singles Week event is another instance of Moira's grandiosity.
jokes.explanation: 'Frippit' is a great Moira-ism — her language is one of the show's ongoing jokes and this one lands well. 'Media training' as a legitimate concern for a motel singles event is the Moira grandiosity gag at its most compact.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Hi everyone, welcome to our singles event. I'm so excited to be here... as a single person. Which I am. Definitely single. Very, very single.

jokes.text: Wait, Jocelyn's driving? / Yes, Alexis! She insisted! My nerves are fried!
jokes.setup: Moira says Jocelyn is 'pulling up to the hospital right now.'
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the laboring Jocelyn drove herself — and that Moira's nerves are what she's concerned about — flips the expected concern hierarchy.
jokes.explanation: Two-layer joke: (1) Jocelyn driving herself to the hospital while in labor is absurd and funny, and (2) Moira claiming her OWN nerves are fried flips the concern to the wrong person. Good escalation and character integration for Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I used to have a very specific type. You know, like a lock, and the key had to be this really specific shape. It had to be tall, it had to be European, preferably Adriatic royalty...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But then I realized I was being way too picky, so I just started accepting any key that fit.

jokes.text: I think that is a service that could benefit all of us.
jokes.setup: Someone has requested 'Do Not Disturb' signs for Singles Week.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry implication — that she too would benefit from not being disturbed, regardless of the romantic context — is delivered with perfect flatness.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's signature deadpan at its most efficient. The double meaning — Do Not Disturb signs for romance vs. Stevie's general desire to be left alone — is a tight, clean joke that's perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He had to be willing to live off the grid, have at least three dogs, and be comfortable with the fact that I would never be comfortable.

jokes.text: Is that you? / No, my phone's in my pocket.
jokes.setup: A phone is ringing/buzzing somewhere in the motel.
jokes.punchline: The exchange implies the sound might be mistaken for a bodily function, or that Stevie's question is ambiguous enough that Johnny clarifies it's a phone — both responses are funnier for the brevity.
jokes.explanation: A quick, mild joke about an ambiguous phone sound. It's efficient and gets a chuckle but doesn't reveal much about character. The humor is mostly in the ambiguity of 'is that you?' — functional comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I threw away someone because they were too nice.

jokes.text: Roland, it's happening. And then baby. And then, like a whole row of baby emojis, and an explosion.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie have found Roland's phone and are reading Jocelyn's missed texts about being in labor.
jokes.punchline: Stevie reading Jocelyn's texts aloud — escalating from urgent message to emoji string to 'an explosion' — is a perfect comedic reading of how people text in crises.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from earnest labor alert to emoji storm to 'an explosion' is a precise and very funny observation about how people communicate via text in emotionally heightened moments. Stevie's flat delivery of the emoji description is excellent. 'An explosion' as a description of a birth emoji sequence is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis Rose
dialogue.line: Welcome everyone to our singles event. So, you're all here because you're looking for love, which is great, but I want you to know that love isn't just about finding another person. It's about finding yourself first. And I've learned that the hard way. You see, I spent so much of my life trying to be what other people wanted me to be, and I lost myself in the process. And then I realized that I was so afraid of being alone that I would do anything to avoid it. But being alone isn't the worst thing. The worst thing is being with someone and still feeling alone. So tonight, I want you all to really think about what you're looking for, and more importantly, who you are.

dialogue.speaker: Singles event attendee
dialogue.line: So... is there a sign-in sheet or...?

jokes.text: Well we-we didn't do anything wrong here. Right? / I didn't. / You're the one who put his phone on silent. / Because you didn't know how to.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie realize Roland missed Jocelyn's labor calls because they silenced his phone.
jokes.punchline: Their rapid blame-deflection at each other — culminating in 'Because you didn't know how to' — is a classic double act of guilt-shifting.
jokes.explanation: Good escalating blame exchange — the payoff of the earlier 'do you know how to do that, Stevie?' joke. The callback makes this land better than it would in isolation. Functional sitcom mechanics executed well.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh God, the hammer's about to drop.

jokes.text: We gave him a lot of errands to run.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie have established that Roland missed his wife's labor because they gave him errands and silenced his phone, and Johnny says he'll probably be 'back soon.'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's quiet acknowledgment that they may have materially delayed Roland's arrival at the birth is delivered as a mild afterthought.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's dawning guilt delivered with maximum understatement. 'We gave him a lot of errands to run' as the quiet horror of what they've done is very Johnny Rose — self-aware but too polite to state it directly. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I have some bad news. We've lost the phone.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What? How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not entirely sure, but it's gone.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Gone? GONE?! Do you understand what that means? That's our only line of communication with the outside world!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Where could it be? Where did you last see it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, I—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, no. We need to find it. NOW. Check the back office. Check the storage room. Check everywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God—wait. Wait, is that it?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: That's the phone. That's been the phone the whole time. It's right here on the counter.

jokes.text: It's always gifts for the dogs, and never for me. / Definitely one of my pet peeves.
jokes.setup: David delivers dog sweaters for the pet adoption event and Ted jokes that the dogs always get gifts.
jokes.punchline: David immediately replies that it's 'one of my pet peeves' — with 'pet' doing double duty as a pun in a scene involving a veterinarian and dog sweaters.
jokes.explanation: A clean pun that works in context — 'pet peeves' in a vet's office is timely and the setup (Ted complaining about dogs getting gifts) makes it feel earned rather than shoehorned. The delivery matters; David saying it without fanfare is funnier than if it were flagged.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, Bob, he is quite slippery, isn't he?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm standing right here, Johnny.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, I just... I just was expecting Alexis, so you showing up here has really thrown me off my game. Um... no, your game is... tight.
jokes.setup: Ted admits David's presence has thrown him off his game.
jokes.punchline: David's instinctive reassurance — 'no, your game is... tight' — delivered with uncertainty as he searches for the right word, is simultaneously kind and awkward.
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'tight' — David reaching for the compliment — is the joke. He's trying to be reassuring in a genuinely awkward situation and lands on a slightly odd word choice. The awkwardness of the exchange is well-managed, showing David outside his comfort zone attempting genuine kindness.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I was on a dirt bike in Mexico, just riding through the desert, wind in my hair, and I realized I'd never felt more alive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know exactly what you mean. I was on a dirt bike once.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, no. But I was in a very uncomfortable Uber.

jokes.text: Yes. No, that is an emotional depth that is very, very out of character for her. So, I can understand why that would be weird for you.
jokes.setup: Ted is processing Alexis saying 'I love you,' and David is acknowledging it's surprising.
jokes.punchline: David confirms it's 'very, very out of character for her' — a backhanded assessment of his sister that's technically supportive of Ted's confusion but also deeply uncharitable to Alexis.
jokes.explanation: David's sibling ambivalence — validating Ted's confusion by confirming Alexis is emotionally shallow — is character-consistent and gently funny. 'Emotional depth that is very, very out of character' is both funny and a little mean, which is David's mode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I understand. I too have been burned in relationships. I'm like a marshmallow — a hard candy shell on the outside, but on the inside, I'm just a soft, gooey center that gets destroyed the moment someone applies heat.

jokes.text: I broke up with Heather. / So I should probably stay, then, for a minute?
jokes.setup: David has been edging toward the exit after the awkward conversation.
jokes.punchline: Ted's bombshell revelation stops David mid-exit, and David's immediate pivot — from escaping to staying — is delivered as a mild logistical adjustment.
jokes.explanation: 'So I should probably stay, then, for a minute?' is an excellent line — David processing a major romantic development through the lens of social obligation ('probably,' 'for a minute') while his face presumably shows much more. The understatement is perfect for David and the structure of the exchange is crisp.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I brought you something as a thank you for everything you do for the business.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Oh, how thoughtful! What is it?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a treat. I thought you'd enjoy it.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Mmm, this is... actually quite good. What flavor is this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's... well, it's a dog biscuit.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A dog biscuit. For the dog. Except I thought it would be funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: David, I just ate a dog biscuit.

jokes.text: Okay, just triple checking, because the website acts as a directory. / And a message board, we know.
jokes.setup: Alexis is stalling before the Singles Week event, checking that everyone registered on the website.
jokes.punchline: A waiting reporter finishes Alexis's sentence for her — they've clearly heard this spiel before — expressing impatience with perfect deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Good crowd-impatience gag — the reporter completing Alexis's sentence shows she's been over-explaining the website. It also reveals that Alexis is nervous and stalling. Efficient and funny without being exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Woman in labor
dialogue.line: I don't know who Alexis is, but if she's here, I trust her completely. She has this energy about her—this calm, capable energy. When she looks at you, you feel like everything is going to be okay. She's probably the most competent person I've ever met.

jokes.text: Sorry, when is this gonna start? There is a seniors' craft fair that I have been asked to cover in Elmglen, but as a single woman who drove 20 miles to be here, I'd rather write about this.
jokes.setup: The reporters are waiting impatiently for the Singles Week event to begin.
jokes.punchline: The reporter reveals she's also single and drove 20 miles — blurring the line between professional journalist and event participant, exposing her personal investment.
jokes.explanation: The reporter's personal stakes — she's a single woman covering a singles event — is a lovely irony. 'But as a single woman who drove 20 miles to be here' undercuts her journalistic neutrality in a very human way. Good observational comedy about professional/personal overlap.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Moira, step aside. I got this.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But Roland, I was just—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You did great, really. But I'm here now. You can go.

jokes.text: Hey, singles, I'm Alexis Rose, and I, like all of you, am single.
jokes.setup: Alexis begins her keynote address, identifying herself as a speaker.
jokes.punchline: The pause and the addition of 'like all of you, am single' reveals this is partly Alexis speaking to herself — she's using the event for her own processing.
jokes.explanation: The slight discomfort of Alexis reminding herself — and the room — that she is single, after the breakup with Ted, gives this a bittersweet comedic quality. The pause before 'like all of you, am single' is doing real work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's the epidural talking, dear. By next week you won't remember any of this.

jokes.text: Now, I used to think that my one special lock needed to be like super aggressive, and come from a long line of Adriatic royalty, or have a beard, and be really into the woods.
jokes.setup: Alexis is delivering a metaphor-driven keynote about love, using the lock-and-key metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Her description of her 'ideal lock' — 'super aggressive,' 'Adriatic royalty,' 'beard,' 'really into the woods' — is a comedic biography of her past terrible romantic choices wrapped in metaphor.
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely excellent line. The specificity of the requirements — 'Adriatic royalty' and 'really into the woods' as parallel qualifications — perfectly lampoons Alexis's past romantic catastrophes while delivering them through an earnest metaphor speech. The comedy comes from how precise and autobiographical it is. 'Adriatic royalty' in particular is a perfect absurdist detail. High character integration, high originality, highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, David really knows what desperate singles want.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know if that's a compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's not not a compliment.

jokes.text: Then I realized that sometimes, a special little lock can come along, that you didn't think would fit because it didn't look, or act, like the lock you thought you needed. So instead of actually giving it a real chance, you throw it away because it was too nice. And it liked you too much, and cared.
jokes.setup: Alexis has pivoted from her absurd romantic history to describing Ted in metaphor.
jokes.punchline: The audience (and the viewer) realizes this is a real-time processing of her heartbreak over Ted — 'too nice,' 'liked you too much, and cared' as the disqualifying factors is painfully self-aware.
jokes.explanation: This is the comedic/emotional peak of Alexis's speech — the cringe-comedy of watching someone deliver a very thinly veiled personal confession to a room of strangers. 'Too nice' and 'liked you too much, and cared' as relationship dealbreakers is both funny and devastating, and the specificity makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It went well.

jokes.text: So, are we playing a game, or not?
jokes.setup: Alexis has delivered a very personal, meandering lock-and-key metaphor speech that has clearly gone off the rails.
jokes.punchline: An audience member cuts through it all with the most practical possible question — deflating the emotional speech with logistical impatience.
jokes.explanation: Perfect comic deflation. After Alexis's extended heartbreak metaphor, a single practical question punctures it entirely. The economy is exceptional — five words that say everything. The structure is excellent because the longer and more emotional Alexis's speech, the harder this lands. This is the kind of joke that earns its laugh through patience.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I love you too. And I don't say that lightly. I also don't say it like Mariah Carey, so you know it's real.

jokes.text: Apparently they took Roland's phone away from him. / I don't know! / Well... who would do that to a man whose wife is just about to go into labor?
jokes.setup: Moira calls the motel from the hospital, having realized Roland is out of contact.
jokes.punchline: Moira demands to know who would take Roland's phone — not knowing it was Johnny and Stevie — while Johnny and Stevie are exactly those people, standing right there.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony of Moira indicting the exact people she's talking to works well. 'Who would do that to a man whose wife is just about to go into labor?' is the right level of rhetorical outrage. The callback to the phone-silencing pays off here.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm going to get you the perfect tea. I'm going to find out what temperature water should be, what kind of leaves, what kind of cup. I'm going to make this the most perfect tea moment of your life.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, you don't have to—

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No. You said tea. I'm getting you tea.

jokes.text: Well, in our defense, Jocelyn's a few days early.
jokes.setup: Moira is asking who would take Roland's phone while his wife is about to give birth.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's mild defense — that the baby is early, therefore he couldn't have anticipated it — is a fundamentally weak alibi delivered with straight-faced sincerity.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully calibrated Johnny Rose moment. 'In our defense, Jocelyn's a few days early' is the exact kind of technically-true-but-morally-bankrupt defense that Johnny deploys when cornered. Economy is excellent — the joke is over before you fully process how bad the excuse is, which is part of why it lands.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I think I actually met someone!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Really? That's great.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, we talked all night at the singles event. He was so charming and interested in everything I had to say.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what's the catch?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: There's no catch. He works in media, he's super ambitious, and he kept asking me all these questions about my family and—oh God.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just realized he was interviewing me. For a story.

jokes.text: Is this it? / Yeah, you're a lifesaver, Johnny.
jokes.setup: Roland is frantically looking for his phone to call Jocelyn after learning she's in labor.
jokes.punchline: Johnny retrieves the phone (that he/Stevie silenced), hands it back, and Roland calls him a 'lifesaver' — not knowing Johnny caused the problem.
jokes.explanation: Johnny being called a 'lifesaver' by the person whose phone he silenced and whose wife's labor he nearly missed is a good irony beat. Solid callback payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, look at them! They're so in love. It's like they just met and they already know they're soulmates.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, those are Janet and Kevin from the newspaper. They've been trying to destroy each other's careers for three years.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, what? They're rivals?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Bitter rivals. They wrote a 47-part exposé about each other last month.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But they're literally holding hands and whispering sweet nothings.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I know. It's deeply unsettling. I think they've gone insane.

jokes.text: Yeah wh... snake? / Is a little harsh, but he's definitely slippery, I'll give ya that. / I'm standing right here, Bob.
jokes.setup: Johnny is on the phone with Bob (Roland's friend) after Roland has left for the hospital.
jokes.punchline: Bob has apparently called Johnny a 'snake'; Johnny half-defends himself ('slippery' is a concession), then notes he's right there on the call — a mild, resigned dignity.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's half-concession — 'slippery, I'll give ya that' — is a lovely moment of self-aware guilt. He can't fully defend himself because he knows he's sort of in the wrong, so he negotiates downward on the insult. 'I'm standing right here, Bob' as the last word is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You really outdid yourself tonight, darling. The event was absolutely flawless.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, thank you! Though I have to say, I learned from the best. Remember when you planned that gala and the ice sculpture melted into the shrimp display?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That was a freak occurrence.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, a freak occurrence that you somehow still blamed on the venue.

jokes.text: Yeah, that sounds very dark. / You know how many off-road dirt bike tours I had to take, just to get her outta my head? / Shockingly, never been on a dirt bike. Um... / But I'm sure like a lot-a lot... A lot of tours, David.
jokes.setup: Ted is describing how he spent two weeks alone in Mexico on what was supposed to be his honeymoon with Alexis.
jokes.punchline: David reacts to the dirt bike tours with 'shockingly, never been on a dirt bike' — characterizing himself as the kind of person for whom dirt bikes are unimaginable — then immediately pivots to validating Ted's number of tours.
jokes.explanation: 'Shockingly, never been on a dirt bike' is excellent David — the 'shockingly' is doing a lot of work (the shock being how anyone could be surprised by this fact). His pivot to 'A lot of tours, David' shows David's empathy developing in real time. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Look at him. He's got my nose, my chin... he looks just like me. And somehow he's still handsome!

jokes.text: Okay, look, um, I would hardly call myself an expert on this subject, and by subject I mean genuine human emotion.
jokes.setup: David is about to give Ted romantic advice
jokes.punchline: David clarifies that his inexpertise is specifically in 'genuine human emotion' — acknowledging his own emotional limitations as a preamble to giving advice about them
jokes.explanation: 'By subject I mean genuine human emotion' is a beautifully self-aware David line — he's acknowledging that emotional literacy is specifically his weak suit while setting up to give emotional advice. The specificity of the clarification ('genuine human') is the joke. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: They named the baby 'Roland Moira Schitt'.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Roland Moira Schitt? That's a terrible name.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, we wanted to honor both of you.

jokes.text: Um, have we met? I've been burned so many times, I'm basically the human equivalent of the inside of a roasted marshmallow.
jokes.setup: David has told Ted he knows about being burned in relationships.
jokes.punchline: The marshmallow metaphor — charred outside but gooey inside — is simultaneously self-deprecating, genuinely poetic, and very David in its specificity.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best lines in the episode. 'Human equivalent of the inside of a roasted marshmallow' is a metaphor that works on multiple levels: it's visually specific, it captures emotional damage in an unexpected way (burned outside, soft inside — vulnerable despite the damage), and it sounds exactly like something David Rose would say. The 'Um, have we met?' setup is also good. Highly quotable and shows genuine writing craft.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that is... a name. And how thoughtful that you've named her after me. Although I have to say, 'Ronnie' has never been on my list of names I'd hoped to pass down.

jokes.text: Maybe this can be your thank you. / That's a dog treat? / Looks like a real treat.
jokes.setup: David has given Ted an encouraging speech about going after Alexis.
jokes.punchline: Ted offers David a 'treat' — which turns out to be a dog treat — with the pun 'looks like a real treat' playing on both meanings.
jokes.explanation: A vet giving someone a dog treat as a 'thank you' is a decent visual gag with a pun attached. The pun is telegraphed but delivered well. Ted's vet humor is a running character note.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Come on, Moira, just hold her for a second.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, no, no. Roland, I don't—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: She's named after you! Your namesake!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no!

jokes.text: No, no, Jocelyn, normally I wouldn't even consider leaving, but Alexis... Alexis has never handled this kind of responsibility. And I would just hate to see it all come clattering down around her.
jokes.setup: Moira is at the hospital with Jocelyn in labor, trying to justify leaving to attend the Singles Week event.
jokes.punchline: Moira's concern is framed as maternal worry, but the content reveals she doesn't trust Alexis and primarily wants to run the event herself.
jokes.explanation: 'Come clattering down around her' is a lovely Moira-ism — theatrical language for an event she's actually using as cover for her own ambitions. Consistent character comedy.

jokes.text: Listen, I can't speak to who Alexis was before you came here, and from what I've heard I don't really wanna dig any deeper into that...
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is defending Alexis to Moira
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's caveat — 'from what I've heard I don't really wanna dig any deeper into that' — acknowledges Alexis's wild pre-Schitt's Creek history with a politely horrified aside
jokes.explanation: A nice joke that does world-building through implication — we understand that what Jocelyn has heard about pre-Schitt's Creek Alexis is alarming, and she's actively choosing not to know more. Delivered while in labor, which makes her composure even funnier.

jokes.text: She is a hard worker, she is kind, and she is capable of so much more than people give her credit for. / Jocelyn, that is one of the most generous compliments one could receive as a mother.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is defending Alexis to Moira while in labor.
jokes.punchline: Moira receives a compliment about HER DAUGHTER as if it's a compliment about herself — 'one could receive as a mother' centers Moira's feelings in a speech about Alexis.
jokes.explanation: Moira making Jocelyn's compliment about Alexis into a compliment about herself as a mother is a beautifully precise character beat. It's narcissistic but also oddly touching — Moira does feel pride in Alexis, even if she can only receive it through herself. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Oh, if the drugs are any good, you will.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn tells Moira 'I will never forget this' for staying with her during labor.
jokes.punchline: Moira's parting shot — implying the epidural will erase Jocelyn's memory of the moment — is dark, funny, and delivered as Moira sweeps out.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best lines of the episode. Moira's exit line — 'if the drugs are any good, you will' — is simultaneously dark (undermining the sentimental moment), funny (practical pharmaceutical observation), and perfectly in character (Moira can never let a sincere moment land without a twist). The economy is exceptional. It's delivered as she's already leaving, which makes it land harder. The line is quintessentially Moira.

jokes.text: Well, David, I gotta hand it to you. We've already sold half of our massage oils. It's like you're an oracle when it comes to the sex lives of lonely people.
jokes.setup: The Singles Week merch is selling well.
jokes.punchline: 'Oracle when it comes to the sex lives of lonely people' is a specific and funny way to compliment David's retail instincts.
jokes.explanation: 'Oracle when it comes to the sex lives of lonely people' is a quotable line with good economy. Patrick framing David's retail skills as a gift for predicting the desires of the lonely is affectionate and specific.

jokes.text: How'd it go with Ted? [David turns, looks at Patrick] I love you.
jokes.setup: Patrick casually asks how the Ted visit went
jokes.punchline: David's answer to 'how'd it go?' is simply 'I love you' — confirming in two words that (1) it went well enough to prompt this, and (2) David is now saying it back for the first time
jokes.explanation: The episode's second peak moment. Patrick asks a simple question about Ted; David answers it with 'I love you' — the best possible answer. The joke is structural and emotional simultaneously: the non-answer that IS the answer. The callback to joke #22 earns maximum emotional/comedic payoff. On rewatch you realize David went to Ted specifically because Patrick's 'I love you' made him brave enough to advocate for love. The economy is perfect: two words close the episode's emotional arc. Approaches the caliber of the 9.5 anchor.

jokes.text: I know I'll never be able to compete with Mariah. / Mm. / It just kind of feels like one of those perfect moments that you dream about. Except in my dream, I'm holding a nice cup of tea. / Oh... / I'm kidding. I don't need the tea. No, I am making this perfect moment, perfect.
jokes.setup: David has just returned 'I love you' and the moment is romantic and weightful.
jokes.punchline: Patrick undercuts his own perfect moment by mentioning he'd dreamed of holding tea, then — after David reacts with mock hurt — walks it back and goes to get tea anyway, committing to making it perfect.
jokes.explanation: This sequence does several things at once: callbacks the Mariah line (acknowledging he can't compete), undercuts the romantic moment with the tea detail (classic Patrick self-sabotage of perfection), then corrects it by committing to get the tea anyway. 'No, I am making this perfect moment, perfect' is a great line — the doubled 'perfect' signals his total sincerity. The tea callback (from David's earlier request) rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Hey, it's musical chairs. It's a simple concept. Ugh! Keep moving! People are clustering, and you don't want that!
jokes.setup: Alexis is running the modified singles event games after her keynote speech.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's exasperation at adults not understanding musical chairs — 'people are clustering, and you don't want that' — is a funny image of someone taking a frivolous task extremely seriously.
jokes.explanation: Alexis as a frustrated singles event wrangler is a good character image. 'People are clustering, and you don't want that' applied to musical chairs among single adults is slightly absurd in a fun way.

jokes.text: Um, actually you may not be able to tell by the way those two are gazing into each other's eyes, they actually work for rival newspapers.
jokes.setup: Moira has arrived and is looking at the successful event.
jokes.punchline: Alexis points out that two people who appear to be connecting romantically are actually rival journalists — the event has created unexpected cross-professional chemistry.
jokes.explanation: A nice payoff for the journalist-as-participant setup earlier. The irony of rivals gazing at each other romantically is a clean joke about love overcoming professional competition. Also callbacks the earlier reporter who admitted she was single.

jokes.text: Um, actually you may not be able to tell by the way those two are gazing into each other's eyes, they actually work for rival newspapers.
jokes.setup: Moira notes that the press has left
jokes.punchline: Alexis has inadvertently matched two rival newspaper reporters — who are now gazing into each other's eyes. Singles Week is working, just not as intended
jokes.explanation: A nice observational joke — the press Moira wanted has stayed because they're falling for each other. Alexis delivers this with complete deadpan. The detail that they're rivals adds an extra layer.

jokes.text: Seems there's a lot more of me in you, than I give you credit for. / Lucky me.
jokes.setup: Moira is apologizing for underestimating Alexis and trying to give her a compliment.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'compliment' centers herself — it's really about how great Moira is that Alexis has absorbed so much of her — and Alexis's deadpan 'Lucky me' deflects it perfectly.
jokes.explanation: This is a perfectly calibrated mother-daughter exchange. Moira's apology turns into self-congratulation (she's complimenting Alexis for containing Moira), and Alexis's 'Lucky me' is two words of pure deadpan that say everything. The character integration for both is excellent — only these two people in this relationship would have this exact exchange.

jokes.text: I'll take it from here. / I thought you were just apologizing for underestimating me? / You can't play the game with a clipboard in your hand. Go on.
jokes.setup: Moira has just apologized to Alexis and given her a backhanded compliment.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately reasserts control of the event she was just admiring Alexis for running — the apology lasted approximately 20 seconds.
jokes.explanation: Moira's apology expiring the moment the event needs running is a perfect character beat. 'You can't play the game with a clipboard in your hand' is delivered as genuine wisdom while also being a takeover, which is pure Moira. Alexis's callback question ('I thought you were just apologizing?') is also funny.

jokes.text: Hi. / Apparently I only have two minutes, so I'm gonna make this quick. / Hi. / I'm Ted, I'm newly single.
jokes.setup: Alexis is participating in the speed-dating musical chairs she set up. Ted sits down across from her.
jokes.punchline: Ted's formal intro — 'I'm Ted, I'm newly single' — mirrors the event format while delivering the emotional payload of his breakup with Heather in the most matter-of-fact way.
jokes.explanation: Ted using the formal singles event introduction ('I'm Ted, I'm newly single') to deliver his romantic declaration to Alexis is a beautifully constructed moment. It's funny because he's following the protocol of an event she created, sweet because it's the setup to a kiss, and 'newly single' does enormous work in two words. The structure — treating the most significant personal revelation as a speed-dating intro — is excellent.

jokes.text: And I'd be kidding myself if I told you that I haven't wanted to do this every single day for the last two years. / Do what?
jokes.setup: Ted has given his romantic speech about wanting to do 'this' every day for two years.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's innocent 'Do what?' — either genuine confusion or deliberate obliviousness — forces Ted to show rather than tell, leading to the kiss.
jokes.explanation: 'Do what?' is a perfect Alexis beat — it could be genuine obliviousness or calculated innocence, and either reading is funny. The line forces the action to speak for itself (the kiss), which is structurally elegant. Two words that either reveal character or beautifully play dumb. High economy score.

jokes.text: Oh! Oh, no, no thank you. [Alexis reacts to being kissed in front of the assembled singles]
jokes.setup: Ted has just kissed Alexis in front of the entire assembled Singles Week crowd.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's flustered 'Oh no no thank you' — as if declining a canapé — is the wrong social register for the moment, and the audience presumably watches.
jokes.explanation: 'Oh no no thank you' as a response to being kissed is a quintessentially Alexis moment — polite social reflex kicking in before her actual feelings do. The register mismatch (treating a romantic kiss like someone offering her something she doesn't want) is very funny and specific to her character.

jokes.text: Oh, what a handsome guy. He looks just like me. And yet, he's handsome.
jokes.setup: Roland is looking at his newborn son for the first time.
jokes.punchline: Roland acknowledges the baby looks like him, then immediately concedes the baby is handsome — implying Roland does NOT think he is handsome himself.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent self-deprecating joke with perfect structure. The setup ('he looks just like me') creates the expectation of proud parental narcissism; the pivot ('and yet, he's handsome') immediately subverts it by Roland acknowledging he himself is not handsome. The 'and yet' is doing all the comedic work. Economical, self-aware, and executed with great timing. High quotability.

jokes.text: So, we'd like you to say hello to Roland Moira Schitt.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn are thanking Moira for staying at the hospital
jokes.punchline: The baby's name is 'Roland Moira Schitt' — which means the child's name when said aloud sounds like 'Roland Moira Schitt' — the last name doing unspeakable things to the middle name
jokes.explanation: The episode's biggest structural joke. 'Roland Moira Schitt' — the name is both an honor (Moira) and a curse (Schitt). The full name said aloud creates the obvious toilet-related reading ('Moira Schitt'). It's a long-game setup paid off with perfect timing. Moira's reaction ('Wow, that's... the name is really something, isn't it?') confirms she gets it. Highly impactful, genuinely original as a naming joke.

jokes.text: So, we'd like you to say hello to Roland Moira Schitt.
jokes.setup: The Schitts tell Moira they want to thank her for staying through Jocelyn's labor.
jokes.punchline: The baby's full name — Roland Moira Schitt — is the reward/punchline: Moira's name combined with 'Schitt' creates an unexpectedly unfortunate combination.
jokes.explanation: 'Roland Moira Schitt' is a great reveal — the name is both an honor (naming the baby after Moira) and a curse (Moira Schitt is an unfortunate combination). Moira's reaction — 'Wow, that's... the name is really something, isn't it?' — is the button. The joke has been earned by the episode's emotional arc and lands as a warm, funny conclusion.

jokes.text: Wow, that's... the name is really something, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Moira has just been told the baby is named Roland Moira Schitt.
jokes.punchline: Moira's understated reaction — 'the name is really something' — is the diplomatic way of acknowledging that 'Moira Schitt' is not a gift to put on a birth certificate.
jokes.explanation: Moira's response is perfectly calibrated — she can't say 'Moira Schitt is a terrible name' because they're trying to honor her, so 'the name is really something' does the diplomatic work while the audience understands exactly what she means. High character integration: only Moira would find this exact register of gracious horror.

jokes.text: And an honor I dream not of!
jokes.setup: Moira is processing being honored by having a baby named after her — specifically, 'Moira Schitt.'
jokes.punchline: Moira inverts the expected grateful response ('an honor I dream of!') to signal this is not the legacy she imagined, while still maintaining the veneer of gratitude.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully economical Moira line. The grammatical inversion ('dream not of' rather than 'don't dream of') is pure Moira-speak, and the content — refusing to claim this honor while technically not being rude — is perfect character comedy. 'An honor I dream not of!' manages to be both dismissive and darkly funny while staying within the bounds of the moment. Highly quotable and deeply Moira.

jokes.text: Look at that, the two Moira's. / Take him, John, my hands are shaking. / No, thank you.
jokes.setup: Moira is holding baby Roland Moira Schitt.
jokes.punchline: Moira's hands shake with what could be emotion or horror, she asks Johnny to take the baby, and Johnny — equally uncomfortable with other people's babies — simply says 'No, thank you.'
jokes.explanation: Johnny's 'No, thank you' as a response to being handed a baby is a great closing beat — completely in character for both Roses, who are out of their depth with genuine emotion and babies. The 'two Moiras' setup from Roland makes this a warm-funny final image. Economy is excellent.

tmdb_overview: Singles Week gets underway just as Jocelyn’s baby arrives, and Patrick takes his relationship with David to the next level.
insight: Cringe escalation drives a mid-tier episode as character comedy stretches awkwardness thin.
